slides.title: Introduction to Go (Golang)
slides.subtitle:
Simplicity, Reliability, and Efficiency
Modern Systems Programming

slides.title: 1. Introduction & History
slides.subtitle: Why Go was created and what it stands for

list.text: Static Typing - Provides safety and catches errors early at compile time.
Garbage Collected - Relieves developers from manual memory management, preventing memory leaks.
Compiled - Direct translation to machine code means no virtual machine overhead, leading to blazing fast execution.

box.title: Go (often called Golang) is a statically typed, compiled programming language designed at Google. It was created to address the challenges of large-scale software development: slow builds, uncontrolled dependencies, and difficult concurrency. Go combines the performance of low-level languages like C++ with the readability and productivity of high-level languages like Python.
Created at Google in 2007 to solve real engineering problems

note.text: Released as open source in November 2009

list.text: Composition over Inheritance: Go avoids complex class hierarchies, preferring small, focused interfaces and struct embedding.
Explicit over Implicit: No hidden magic. Errors are values, and control flow is always clear.
Speed: Fast enough to feel like an interpreted language, but with the safety of static typing.

box.title: Go's design philosophy is centered around the idea that clever code is harder to maintain than simple code. By providing a small number of orthogonal features that work well together, Go reduces the cognitive load on developers, making it easier to read and understand large codebases even years after they were written.

list.text: Strongly Typed: No implicit type conversions (e.g., cannot add int to float64 directly).
Type Inference: The := operator allows the compiler to infer types, reducing boilerplate.
Structural Typing: Interfaces are satisfied implicitly, promoting decoupling.

box.title: Go features a pragmatic type system that balances the safety of static typing with the flexibility of dynamic languages. It is designed to be efficient during compilation while providing the power needed for modern software architecture.
The Go Gopher is one of the most beloved mascots in tech

slides.title: 2. Environment & Tooling
slides.subtitle: Setting up your workspace for success

box.title: Quick Start

list.text: Select installer for Windows, Mac, or Linux
Legacy (GOPATH): Strictly required code to be at $GOPATH/src.
Modern (Modules): Dependencies are tracked per project, ensuring reproducible builds.
Command: Initialize with go mod init to start a new project.

box.title:
Before version 1.11, projects had to live inside a single global workspace defined by the GOPATH environment variable. This was restrictive and made versioning difficult.

Modern Go uses Go Modules (introduced in 1.11 and default since 1.13), which allows you to place your project anywhere and manage dependencies precisely with a go.mod file.

list.text: go run - Compiles and executes code in one step (ideal for dev).
go build - Generates a single, statically-linked binary with no external dependencies.
go test - Executes unit tests and benchmarks automatically.
go fmt - Re-formats your code according to official Go standards.
go get - Downloads and installs external dependencies.

box.title: Go comes 'batteries included' with a powerful command-line tool that handles everything from building to testing and formatting. This 'one tool to rule them all' approach is a major factor in Go's developer productivity.

Every Go file starts with a package declaration. The main package is a special name that tells the Go compiler that this file should compile as an executable, rather than a shared library. The main function inside this package is the entry point where execution begins.

list.text: package main - Identifies the file as an executable program.
import "fmt" - Provides functions for formatting text and printing to console.
func main() - The mandatory starting point for any Go application.

box.title: Go is strongly typed, but it offers several ways to declare variables to reduce verbosity. While var is used for general declarations, the short declaration operator := is the most common way to initialize variables within functions.

note.text: Note: := cannot be used outside of function bodies.

list.text: Reference Types (pointers, slices, maps, channels): Initialized to nil.

box.title: Unlike many languages where uninitialized variables hold 'garbage' data leading to unpredictable crashes, Go guarantees that every variable is initialized to a predictable zero value. This design choice enhances security and simplifies debugging by ensuring a clean state for every variable.
Go strings are UTF-8 encoded by default

note.text: A rune is a Unicode code point

box.title: Go supports pointers, allowing you to pass references to data rather than copies. However, unlike C/C++, Go pointers are safe: there is no pointer arithmetic, preventing a whole class of memory-related bugs. Pointers are essential for efficient data manipulation and for methods that need to modify their receiver.

note.text: Remember: & gets the address, * gets the value.

box.title: Go requires explicit conversion
Fixed size, part of the type

note.text: Arrays are rarely used directly; use slices!

box.title: Slices are the most powerful and common data structure in Go. Unlike arrays, which have a fixed size, slices are dynamic and grow as needed. A slice is essentially a descriptor for a contiguous segment of an underlying array.

list.text: Data Pointer: A reference to the first accessible element in the underlying array.
Length (len): The current number of elements visible in the slice.
Capacity (cap): The maximum number of elements the slice can hold before a new, larger array must be allocated.

box.title: Under the hood, a Go slice is a lightweight 24-byte 'header' (on 64-bit systems) that points to an underlying array. Because multiple slices can share the same array, understanding this structure is vital for avoiding unintended side effects when modifying data.

box.code:
parent := []int{10, 20, 30, 40, 50}
child := parent[1:4] // [20, 30, 40]

child[0] = 99
fmt.Println(parent[1]) // Outputs: 99!

// To avoid sharing, use make() and copy()
independent := make([]int, len(child))
copy(independent, child)
box.title: Slicing an existing slice does not copy the data; it creates a new header pointing to the same underlying array. This is extremely efficient but means that changing an element in a sub-slice will be visible in the parent slice.

box.code:
s := make([]int, 5, 10) // len 5, cap 10

copy_s := make([]int, len(s))
copy(copy_s, s)

// Remove element at index i
s = append(s[:i], s[i+1:]...)

box.title: How append() works

list.text: May allocate a new underlying array

box.title: A map is Go's built-in associative data structure (like a dictionary or hash table). It maps keys of one type to values of another. Keys must be 'comparable' types (integers, strings, etc.).

Go maps are not thread-safe. Concurrent read/write access to the same map will cause a fatal runtime crash. For multi-threaded scenarios, you must protect maps using a sync.Mutex or use the specialized sync.Map for specific high-contention use cases.
Collections of fields
Go's if statement is familiar but has a few unique features. Parentheses are not required around the condition, and you can include a short 'initialization' statement before the condition.

note.text: The initialization variable is often used for error checking: if err := doTask(); err != nil

box.title: The only loop construct in Go

Go's switch is more flexible than in most languages. It does not require a 'break' statement because cases do not fall through by default. You can also write a 'switch without a condition', which acts as a cleaner alternative to long if-else-if chains.

box.code:
var i interface{} = 42
switch v := i.(type) {
case int:
    fmt.Println("Integer", v)
case string:
    fmt.Println("String", v)
}

box.title: Functions are first-class citizens in Go. They can be passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and assigned to variables. A function is defined with the func keyword, followed by the name, parameters, and the return type.

Go allows you to name the return variables in the function signature. While this can serve as documentation, it also allows for 'naked' returns. However, naked returns should be used sparingly in long functions as they can harm readability.

slides.title: The Defer Keyword: Modern Resource Management

box.title: The defer statement is one of Go's most elegant features for resource management. It delays the execution of a function until the surrounding function returns. This ensures that cleanup logic is always executed, providing a robust 'finally' equivalent that scales with your code.

note.text: LIFO: Deferred calls are executed in Last-In-First-Out order.

box.code:
i := 0
defer fmt.Println(i) // Will print 0, not 1!
i++
box.title: A crucial detail: the arguments to a deferred function are evaluated immediately, when the defer statement is reached, not when the actual call is executed later.
Go does not have classes, but you can define 'methods' on any type (usually structs). A method is just a function with a special 'receiver' argument between the func keyword and the method name.

list.text: Value Receiver: Operates on a copy. Changes are lost.
Pointer Receiver: Operates on the original memory. Changes persist.
Performance: Pointer receivers avoid expensive stack copying for large structs.

box.title: Choosing between a value and a pointer receiver is a fundamental Go decision. Use Pointer Receivers if you need to modify the data within the method or to avoid copying large structs on every call. Use Value Receivers for small, immutable types where thread safety and consistency are more important.

Go does not support class-based inheritance. Instead, it uses Struct Embedding to allow types to reuse fields and methods of other types. This is a cleaner, more flexible way to build complex data structures by composing smaller, focused components.

box.code:
p1 := Person{"Alice", 25}       // Positional
p2 := Person{Name: "Bob"}       // Named (Age=0)
p3 := new(Person)               // Pointer to zeroed
p4 := &Person{Name: "Charlie"}   // Preferred

box.title: In many languages, you must explicitly state that a class 'implements' an interface. In Go, this is done implicitly. If a type provides all the methods declared in an interface, it is considered to implement that interface automatically. This allows you to define interfaces for code you don't even own, leading to highly decoupled systems.
Smaller interfaces are easier to implement and reuse. Go encourages this by allowing you to 'embed' interfaces into other interfaces to create more complex contracts.

box.code:
var i any
i = 42
i = "hello"
box.title: interface{} (or any) can hold any value

Go encourages handling errors explicitly rather than catching exceptions. Errors are just values that implement the error interface. Functions that can fail usually return an error as their last return value, and it's the caller's responsibility to check it immediately.

Since Go 1.13, you can wrap errors to provide more context while still allowing the caller to inspect the root cause. Using the %w verb with fmt.Errorf creates a wrapped error that can be inspected with errors.Is and errors.As.
Sometimes a simple string isn't enough. You can define custom error structs to carry structured data about the failure, which can then be extracted by the caller using errors.As.

list.text: Recover: Regain control in a deferred function

box.title: Only for unrecoverable situations

list.text: Concurrency: Dealing with many things at once

box.title: Concurrency is not Paralellism

A goroutine is a lightweight thread of execution managed by the Go runtime. Unlike OS threads, which are heavy and have a large fixed stack size, goroutines start with very little memory (about 2KB) and are multiplexed onto a small number of OS threads. This allows you to run hundreds of thousands of goroutines simultaneously without exhausting system resources.

Channels are the pipes that connect concurrent goroutines. You can send values into channels from one goroutine and receive those values into another. By default, channels are unbuffered, meaning they require both the sender and receiver to be ready at the same time for the communication to occur. This provides a natural synchronization point.

box.code:
ch := make(chan int, 2)
ch <- 1
ch <- 2
// ch <- 3 would block
box.title: Asynchronous until capacity is reached

box.code:
select {
case msg1 := <-c1:
    fmt.Println("Received", msg1)
case c2 <- 42:
    fmt.Println("Sent to c2")
default:
    fmt.Println("No activity")
}
box.title: Waiting on multiple channel operations
Synchronizing multiple goroutines

box.code:
var mu sync.Mutex
mu.Lock()
counter++
mu.Unlock()
box.title: Protecting shared state

A common Go pattern is the 'Worker Pool', where a fixed number of goroutines process tasks from a shared channel. This limits resource usage while maximizing throughput, as workers avoid the overhead of constant thread creation.
Using a 'quit' or 'done' channel with a select statement is the standard way to implement graceful shutdowns or cancellations in Go services.

box.code:
go run -race main.go
go test -race ./...
box.title: Find concurrency bugs easily

Go's standard library includes a production-grade HTTP server. It is highly performant because every request is handled in its own goroutine automatically. This makes Go an excellent choice for building microservices and web APIs.

Middleware is a powerful pattern in Go for wrapping HTTP handlers with shared logic like logging, authentication, or metrics. Because functions are first-class citizens, middleware is just a function that takes a http.Handler and returns a new http.Handler.

Struct tags allow you to control exactly how your Go data maps to JSON. You can rename fields, hide sensitive data with -, or hide empty fields with omitempty. For more complex validation, many developers use third-party libraries alongside these tags.

Interacting with the file system is straightforward in Go. You can open files, read their content into memory or stream them, and write new data. Always remember to use defer to ensure files are closed, preventing resource leaks.

The context package is essential for managing timeouts, deadlines, and cancellations across API boundaries and goroutines. It allows you to gracefully stop work when it's no longer needed or taking too long.

Go has testing built into the go command. The idiomatic way to write tests is 'Table-Driven Tests', which use a slice of structs to define multiple test cases, making it easy to add new scenarios without repeating logic.

Go provides top-tier benchmarking tools built into the testing package. You can measure the execution time and memory allocations of your functions, allowing for data-driven performance optimizations.

Introduced in Go 1.18, Fuzzing is a type of automated testing that continuously provides random data to your program to find edge cases or security vulnerabilities that might be missed by manual unit tests.
Generics are perfect for building reusable data structures (Stacks, Queues, Linked Lists) and utility functions (Filter, Map, Reduce) that don't depend on the specific type of data they hold.

You can restrict generic types using interfaces. The comparable constraint is built-in for types that support == and !=, while custom interfaces allow for more specific requirements like 'anything that can be sorted'.
Continue your Go journey with these elite resources:
Go is a pragmatic, powerful language designed for the cloud era. Its simplicity is its greatest strength, enabling teams to build reliable and scalable systems with ease.
